title: Stabilization of Granovskii-Zhedanov scars of the XYZ quantum spin chain via non-Hermitian spin relaxation
abstract: The Granovskii-Zhedanov (GZ) states are exact scar states of the spin-S XYZ chain for S >= 1. As a result, local quantum information encoded in a GZ state remains preserved under the unitary dynamics of the XYZ Hamiltonian; thus, these states evade thermalization and violate ergodicity despite the system being otherwise nonintegrable and chaotic. However, in realistic experimental settings, the realization of an ideal XYZ Hamiltonian is not possible, as perturbations are inevitable. These perturbations ultimately lead to the decay and thermalization of the GZ state. We study the stability and dynamics of GZ states in the presence of generic perturbations and propose physically realistic mechanisms to stabilize them. We show that the product structure of the GZ state allows its lifetime to be enhanced in the presence of an external helical magnetic field, which slows down thermalization but does not prevent it at long times. We further demonstrate that the inclusion of effective non-Hermitian spin relaxation processes can substantially stabilize the GZ states, leading to a nonequilibrium steady state with finite fidelity with GZ state. Such dissipative processes can naturally originate from mechanisms such as Purcell-enhanced spontaneous emission or spin-lattice relaxation in the presence of the helical magnetic field. Using infinite time-evolving block decimation and exact time evolution, we systematically analyze the dynamics and robustness of the GZ states in the perturbed non-Hermitian XYZ model. To connect with experimental platforms, we introduce a Hubbard model that maps onto the XYZ spin system and propose that ring-shaped optical lattices may provide a viable route for realizing and stabilizing GZ states. Finally, we present an equivalent Lindblad description of the effective non-Hermitian dynamics.
summary: This paper tackles the problem of stabilizing fragile GZ scar states in the XYZ quantum spin chain against environmental noise. It demonstrates that introducing engineered non-Hermitian spin relaxation terms can robustly stabilize these states, preventing complete thermalization. The findings provide a theoretical roadmap for realizing and controlling quantum coherence in complex, open quantum systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates how to stabilize Granovskii-Zhedanov (GZ) scar states in the XYZ quantum spin chain against inevitable perturbations that typically cause thermalization.
main_result: The inclusion of effective non-Hermitian spin relaxation processes, which can arise from physical mechanisms like Purcell-enhanced emission, substantially stabilizes the GZ states, leading to a non-equilibrium steady state.
method: The study employs theoretical analysis, including deriving equivalent Lindblad descriptions and analyzing dynamics using techniques like infinite time-evolving block decimation (iTEBD) and fidelity measurements.
model_or_system: The primary system is the spin-S XYZ quantum spin chain. The dynamics are modeled using the XYZ Hamiltonian, augmented by a helical magnetic field term and a non-Hermitian spin relaxation term ($\hat{H}_\lambda$).
key_observables: Fidelity ($F_{GZ}$), which measures the overlap of the time-evolved state with the initial GZ state.
important_parameters: Non-Hermitian strength ($\lambda$), perturbation strengths ($\mu$ for onsite, $t$ for hopping), and the spin quantum number ($S$).
main_assumptions: The ideal XYZ Hamiltonian is not experimentally realizable, necessitating stabilization protocols. The non-Hermitian term is assumed to originate from physical relaxation processes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate fidelity dynamics comparing decay ($\lambda=0$) to saturation ($\lambda>0$) under different perturbations, and phase diagrams mapping stability boundaries in the $(\mu, \lambda)$ and $(t, \lambda)$ parameter spaces.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the GZ state's properties under ideal conditions, then analyzes the decay due to perturbations ($\hat{H}_P$). It introduces stabilization via the helical field ($\hat{H}_h$) and, crucially, the non-Hermitian relaxation ($\hat{H}_\lambda$). The analysis progresses from general theory to specific numerical simulations and finally connects the model to physical realizations like optical lattices and the Lindblad master equation.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the challenge of maintaining fragile quantum coherence in realistic, dissipative environments, which is a central theme in modern quantum simulation and open quantum systems theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Direction of the spin (denoted by the blue arrow) at the n-th site in a GZ state. This schematic illustrates the parameters θn and ϕn appearing in Eq. 3, together with the corresponding expectation value of the components of the local spin operator ˆSn. It also indicates the z′-axis, which is aligned with the local spin direction, such that the spin component along this axis is denoted by Sz′ n . (b) Schematic of the spin relaxation process. In the presence of an external magnetic field |hn| = h, the local degenerate up and down spinon levels split and are separated by an energy gap h. The non-Hermitian term proportional to iλ physically represents spinon decay from the down...

FIG. 2. Numerical plots showing the fidelity as a function of time for different scenarios. In all the simulations, the system is initiated with the GZ state except for the figures (d) and (e). Additionally, in all the simulations, the fidelity of the time-evolved state is measured relative to the GZ state, except for the figure (e). (a) Fidelity dynamics under the Hamiltonian in Eq. 10 with onsite perturbation µ = 0.3, shown for different values of the non-Hermitian strength λ, with the system initialized in the GZ state. (b) Fidelity dynamics under the Hamiltonian in Eq. 10 with hopping perturbation t = 0.3 (and zero onsite perturbation), shown for different values of the non-Hermitian...

FIG. 3. (a) Schematic of the experimental arrangement using an optical lattice. A periodic potential is illustrated, within which the atoms are confined. A site-dependent magnetic field hBn is applied at each site n along the Sz′ n direction, aligned with the local magnetization of the GZ state. This field lifts the spin degeneracy, yielding lower-energy down-spinon (or- ange) and higher-energy up-spinon (blue) states. Coupling to the environment induces relaxation from the down-spinon to the up-spinon state at a rate λ. (b) Schematic of the hopping coefficients in the Hubbard model. In the absence of a mag- netic field, the spin-up and spin-down degrees of freedom are degenerate; they are...

FIG. 4. Lindbladian dynamics governed by the system Hamiltonian ˆH0, in the presence of an onsite perturbation µ = 0.3, and with the Lindblad jump operator defined in Eq. 30. Results are shown for finite system sizes N = 3 (trans- parent lines) and N = 6 (dense lines). The parameter q is fixed to q = 4K(κ)/3, while the remaining system parame- ters are set to κ = 0.5, γ = 0.8, and S = 1. The simulation result for λ = −0.4 and system size N = 3 is not visible, as it lies below the blue dashed curve corresponding to λ = −0.2 for the system size N = 6.

FIG. 5. (a) Stabilization of GZ state using alternative non-Hermitian terms in the presence of global onsite perturbation µ = 0.3. The simulations are done for parameters κ = 0.5, γ = 0.8, q = 4K(κ)/5. (b) The overlap os of the spin-1 states |1111 . . .⟩, |1¯11¯1 . . .⟩, and |1¯1¯11¯1¯1 . . .⟩with the GZ scar subspace for finite periodic chain of length N, is shown as a function of κ, represented by solid lines, dashed lines, and dots, respectively. The different colored plots correspond to the GZ scar subspace for different values of q, which is tuned by varying the parameters N and p, since q = 4pK(κ)
